edges.explanation: Bills 35% higher with 2nd highest IEA prices despite 64% clean generation proves the marginal pricing system prevents consumers benefiting from cheap renewables
Record high bills despite cheap renewables is the direct case for wholesale pricing reform to decouple from gas
High bills from gas price exposure proves the case for domestic clean generation with zero fuel cost
64% clean already shows significant progress but 154g CO2/kWh vs 50g target shows the scale of remaining challenge
Current clean share demonstrates the transition is underway and technically feasible
CfD prices at £61-73/MWh vs Hinkley at £92.50 proves renewables are cheaper per unit on LCOE
68% price decline and subsidy-free pipeline proves renewables are approaching independence from subsidies
Falling CfD prices make accelerated wind and solar deployment increasingly affordable
Hinkley's £92.50 vs renewable £61-73 but with $25-30/MWh system costs proves the cost comparison requires nuance
System integration costs for renewables support maintaining nuclear as dispatchable low-carbon baseload
Resources revised down 83-94% with seismic events and 17% support proves fracking is not viable in the UK
BGS evidence of geological unsuitability directly justifies the permanent fracking ban
IEA, IPCC, CCC, and Nature all stating no new fields is the strongest possible evidence base for incompatibility
International scientific consensus directly justifies no new exploration licences
99.9% scientific consensus directly falsifies the claim that CO2 reduction cannot help climate change
Reform UK's core climate premise is factually false per the entire body of peer-reviewed science
247k renewable jobs growing vs 200k fossil jobs declining shows net positive aggregate employment effect
Growing renewable employment demonstrates the transition creates jobs as well as clean power
Germany's $12bn/yr cost and 2% CO2 reduction from nuclear phaseout is the clearest international evidence that closing nuclear while decarbonising fails
Germany's lesson directly justifies maintaining existing UK nuclear and pursuing standardised SMRs
Germany proving nuclear phaseout is the worst decarbonisation strategy directly contradicts the Green position of phasing out nuclear
France achieving 55g CO2/kWh with nuclear proves the technology delivers low-carbon baseload at scale
French success with standardised designs supports pursuing standardised SMRs rather than bespoke large reactors
£40bn/yr at 4x current rate requiring 'perfect execution' quantifies why 2030 is achievable but a huge challenge
NESO's investment estimate directly sizes the grid infrastructure policy as the highest priority
CfDs paying back £346m during the crisis proves the mechanism protects consumers, contradicting claims that subsidies only cost money
CfD payback mechanism working as price insurance supports maintaining and expanding it as part of pricing reform
£30bn saving potential directly justifies locational pricing as a component of the grid policy
Green Homes Grant delivering 10% of target proves voucher schemes fail and institutional delivery is required
Previous scheme failure directly justifies the National Insulation Service as an institutional delivery body rather than another subsidy scheme
Denmark's 50-year community ownership success is the direct model for mandatory community benefit in UK infrastructure
Denmark proving a wind-dominant domestic system works at scale supports the overall transition direction
Both renewables and nuclear being cost-effective in different roles supports the combined domestic clean system
Grid decarbonisation being achievable supports the accelerated wind and solar deployment policy
The 2030 target requires grid as the highest priority — it's the binding constraint on delivery pace
Fracking not being viable directly supports the permanent ban policy
New extraction being incompatible with net zero supports no new licences
Net positive employment supports renewable acceleration as an economic as well as environmental policy
Wholesale pricing reform depends on the claim that gas price-setting is the structural cause of high bills
Strategic gas reserve depends on the claim that gas as market participant distorts prices beyond its generation share
Wind and solar acceleration depends on the claim that domestic clean power solves cost, security, and emissions simultaneously
Maintaining nuclear depends on the claim that closing it while decarbonising is the worst possible approach
The National Insulation Service depends on the claim that insulation is cheapest generation and only institutional delivery works
The entire transition policy framework depends on climate science being correct — which it is at 99.9% consensus
Restricting onshore wind and solar while tripling offshore contradicts the evidence that all three need unrestricted deployment
Annual North Sea licensing directly contradicts the IEA/IPCC/CCC evidence that new extraction is incompatible with net zero
Doubling onshore, tripling solar, quadrupling offshore directly matches the accelerated renewable deployment policy
No new North Sea licences directly matches the evidence-based fossil fuel policy
Doubling insulation funding is right direction but falls short of the institutional delivery the evidence requires
Broad investment in renewables including tidal, wave, and storage aligns with the diversified clean generation approach
Including nuclear in the Lib Dem energy mix aligns with maintaining nuclear alongside renewables
Emergency insulation programme closely matches the National Insulation Service policy
Insulation-first approach and community ownership closely match the evidence-based demand reduction policy
Scrapping all renewable subsidies contradicts every cost metric showing renewables are cheaper and CfDs protect consumers
Fast-tracking North Sea, enabling fracking, and exploring coal contradicts the entire evidence base on net zero compatibility
Wind and solar deployment is physically blocked without grid infrastructure — grid must lead generation
Wholesale reform and strategic gas reserve work together — removing gas from both price-setting and market participation
Community benefit and ownership builds the public consent that determines whether wind and solar can be deployed at the required pace
Generation investment and demand reduction compete for capital though insulation has higher ROI
£30bn locational pricing savings form part of the broader wholesale pricing reform that decouples bills from gas
Renewables approaching subsidy-free operation means accelerated deployment becomes increasingly self-financing
Mandatory community benefit depends on the Danish precedent showing that community ownership is the mechanism that sustains 50-year policy consensus

cross_domain_edges.explanation: The National Insulation Service is the same programme as the housing domain's national retrofit — energy demand reduction and housing quality improvement are one intervention
Insulation being cheapest generation reinforces the housing finding that retrofit is highest-ROI housing investment
Bills 35% above pre-crisis levels drive fuel poverty that the benefit cap fails to address
The insulation programme depends on the Retrofit Training Academy producing enough skilled installers
£40bn/yr energy transition investment need is part of the total fiscal demand chain constrained by tax locks
Climate change as established science supports the defence domain's recognition of climate as a security threat multiplier
